planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring as the shoots emerge; mountain hosta is one of the largest-growing Hosta species, eventually forming very large clumps in appropriate conditions. Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; mountain hosta is one of the largest species hostas, producing enormous, glossy, rich green, slightly corrugated leaves that can exceed 14 inches in length on nature plants. The species grows naturally at high elevations in Japan on moist, shaded mountain slopes. In the garden, it forms a bold, weed-suppressing mound with a strongly architectural presence. Large white flowers on tall scapes in late summer. The cultivar 'Aureomarginata' features bold yellow to cream margins on the large leaves, creating a dramatic variegated effect. A species of great horticultural importance as a parent of many large-leaved hosta cultivars. In zone 9, mulch well and ensure consistent moisture through the summer heat.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Hosta tardiva is a late-emerging species that does not fully expand until late spring, reducing damage from late frosts.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; tardiva hosta is distinguished by its late bloom time — the fragrant, pale lavender to white flowers appear in late summer to fall, significantly later than most hostas, extending the shade garden's flowering season into September and October. The species name 'tardiva' (late) reflects this late-blooming habit. The medium-sized, glossy, dark green leaves are attractive through the growing season and the plant's late emergence in spring reduces the risk of frost damage to the unfurling leaves. An important parent species in breeding late-season flowering hosta cultivars. The fragrant flowers attract butterflies and hummingbirds to the shade garden in the fall season.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in early spring after flowering; Persian epimedium spreads steadily by underground rhizomes to form a dense, weed-suppressing mat in dry to moist shade. Plant in well-drained, humus-rich soil in partial to deep shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Persian epimedium from the Caucasus and northern Iran is one of the most vigorous and reliable epimediums for shade ground cover, producing cheerful bright yellow flowers in spring and dense, semi-evergreen foliage that smothers weeds effectively. The species is particularly valuable for dry shade under trees where it colonizes aggressively enough to form a solid cover but is not invasively weedy in garden beds. The subspecies subsp. colchicum is especially commonly grown, with larger, brighter yellow flowers and more robust spreading. Epimediums as a genus are among the most underused dry-shade perennials, thriving where other plants struggle. Shear or mow the old foliage in late winter before new growth emerges to show the flowers clearly.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in spring after flowering; Epimedium perralderianum is an Algerian species, one of the most vigorous epimediums for dry shade. Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; Algerian epimedium is native to the Atlas Mountains of northern Algeria and is one of the most drought-tolerant and vigorous ground-covering epimediums available. The bright yellow flowers with distinctive long spurs appear in spring above the bronzy new foliage. The semi-evergreen leaves, which emerge with attractive bronze-red markings and mature to glossy dark green, provide year-round ground cover. It spreads faster than many epimediums, making it effective for quickly covering dry shade areas under deciduous trees. The species has been important in hybridization programs producing vigorous, large-flowered cultivars. A parent of several outstanding garden hybrids. Notable for tolerance of the driest shade conditions — excellent under shallow-rooted trees where little else will grow.

planting.note: Divide rhizomes in early spring; sulphur epimedium prefers well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade. A hybrid between E. pinnatum and E. diphyllum, it combines vigor with attractive semi-evergreen foliage.

directions: Plant in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to deep shade; sulphur epimedium is a naturally occurring or garden hybrid producing pale sulphur-yellow (creamy-yellow) flowers in spring — a softer, more refined color than the bright yellow of E. pinnatum. The semi-evergreen foliage provides attractive ground cover through most of the year in mild climates. Like the best garden epimediums, it spreads steadily by underground rhizomes to form a weed-suppressing colony without becoming invasively aggressive. An excellent choice for naturalizing under mature deciduous trees where the canopy creates challenging dry shade conditions — one of the few perennials that not only survives but thrives in these conditions. Shear back the old foliage in late winter to reveal and enjoy the spring flowers.
